body_txt: Any luxury you can imagine, some rich person already owns it. Maybe even two of it. Conspicuous consumption is any extravagant spending whose main purpose is to show off one's wealth. Sometimes this leads to a vicious cycle: the phrase "keeping up with the Joneses" calls to mind two well-off persons or families trying to out-spend each other to prove themselves, sometimes with Serial Escalation (even if what is bought is Simple, yet Opulent ). A variant of this common among figures of political as well as financial power, such as monarchs, nobles, or merchant lords, is an elaborate collection of exotic and unusual objects, animals, and people which are displayed as proof of your wealth and influence — by gathering together rare things from all over the globe, you display both the connections necessary to find them in the first place and the financial means to secure those things and transport them to your home. Any way you look at it, these people are spending money just because they want to show that they can. You aren't buying a luxury car, you're buying one electroplated with gold. You don't just have a yacht, you have a private aircraft carrier (and not for your private army either). Yes, there can be at least somewhat understandable reasons to spend a lot of money. If you see an expenditure for a reason like these, it's not this trope: This can apply just as often in Real Life as in fiction, but with fiction, some of the spending can even defy reality, thus overlapping with Fiction 500 . Also common among the Nouveau Riche (often leading to A Fool and His New Money Are Soon Parted , since those not used to having money are also not good at understanding how much things cost). It also is often the case that a Mock Millionaire will use Conspicuous Consumption to portray themselves as super-rich; after all, they wouldn't be throwing that kind of money around unless they had a lot more in the bank, right? See Up Marketing , which includes advertising that suggests the more expensive option must be the better one. Sub-tropes Often a sub-trope of Mo' Money, Mo' Problems . Compare Executive Excess , Money Fetish , Upper-Class Twit , Egopolis , Bling of War , and City of Gold for specific forms of extravagance. Contrast Suspicious Spending , which may or may not be extravagant, but goes above and beyond the character's legal income. This trope also goes very well with Awesome, but Impractical , as the stuff that costs the most frequently has the least practical use; note Case in point: the 24-carat gold-plated Macbook in the page image would scratch and dent incredibly easily, as gold is a notoriously soft metal and much less durable than aluminum, which most Macbooks are made of, or plastic, which most other laptops and computers are made from. the point isn't to use it (since anyone can afford something useful), but to show off that you can afford it. See Wicked Wastefulness for when this trope is portrayed as wasteful. Contrast Affluent Ascetic and Bankruptcy Barrel . Compare and contrast Simple, yet Opulent , where an item is obviously high-quality but not ostentatious or tacky. Not to be confused with Crazy Consumption or the Victorian Novel Disease . For purchases that are conspicuous in terms of an investigation, see Suspicious Spending . In economics, this type of product is known as a Veblen good , named after Thorstein Veblen, an American economist who coined the phrase "Conspicuous Consumption" all the way back in 1899 . Such a good sees higher demand by the market the more the price rises, which violates the normal assumed behavior of products.                       noreallife
